---
title: "BonusAssignment"
author: "Somraj Shrestha"
date: "2026-04-09"
output: html_document
---

# 1. Give me notes on the recap material. These questions focus on material that students commonly struggle with (data wrangling, plotting, modeling, or debugging).

# Basic concept and codes on data wrangling

-It is the process of cleaning, transforming, and reshaping data into analysis ready format.

Key packages are dlpyr and tidyr.

# FILTER - subset rows
df %>% filter()

# SELECT - choose columns
df %>% select()


# MUTATE - create/modify columns
df %>% mutate()

# ARRANGE - sort rows
df %>% arrange()

# GROUP_BY + SUMMARISE - aggregate data
df %>% 
  group_by() %>% 
  summarise(),
    n_samples = n(),
    x = sd(y)
  )

# WIDE to LONG
wide_data %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = c(day1, day2, day3),
               names_to = "x",
               values_to = "y")

# LONG to WIDE
long_data %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = x,
              values_from = y)

# Concepts on ggplot
gglot is created on layers and + adds new layer to the plot. 

dataframe (data), aesthetic mapping (x and y variables, shape, color etc.), and a geom (the type of plot) are three things need to create a ggplot.

Faceting helps spliting the plots into small panels based on categorical variable thus, allowing us to compare between the groups within a dataframe.

Alpha adds transparency.

# Modelling
# Simple linear regression
model1 <- lm(y ~ x, data = df)

# Multiple regression
model2 <- lm(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3, data = df)

# With interactions
model3 <- lm(y ~ x1 * x2, data = df)

summary(model1) #to get the summary like p-value, beta and intercept.
anova(model1, model2) #compare the models to identify best fitted models.

# 2. Complete one of the extra optional materials provided, OR explore a topic of your choice, or find a topic online and explore the code and how it works.
Write a short justification (2–3 sentences):
Why did you choose this? What did you want to learn?

-PopGenome was chosen because I will use it for my data analysis. The analysis reveals quantitative measures of genetic differentiation between heat-tolerant and less-tolerant fungal populations, allowing identification of high-FST outlier loci that may be under divergent selection for thermal adaptation. This approach provides both genome-wide population structure insights and pinpoints specific genomic regions potentially associated with heat tolerance traits

# ============================================================================
# PCA ANALYSIS FOR POPULATION STRUCTURE USING SNPRelate
# ============================================================================

# Load required libraries
library(SNPRelate)
library(gdsfmt)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(RColorBrewer)

# ============================================================================
# STEP 1: DATA PREPARATION AND CONVERSION
# ============================================================================

# Convert VCF to GDS format (required for SNPRelate)
# Replace "your_file.vcf" with your actual VCF file path
vcf_file <- "your_file.vcf"
gds_file <- "genome_data.gds"

# Convert VCF to GDS format
snpgdsVCF2GDS(vcf_file, gds_file, method="biallelic.only")

# Open GDS file
genofile <- snpgdsOpen(gds_file)

# ============================================================================
# STEP 2: DATA EXPLORATION
# ============================================================================

# Get basic information about the dataset
cat("Dataset Summary:\n")
cat("Total samples:", length(read.gdsn(index.gdsn(genofile, "sample.id"))), "\n")
cat("Total SNPs:", length(read.gdsn(index.gdsn(genofile, "snp.id"))), "\n")

# Get sample IDs
sample_ids <- read.gdsn(index.gdsn(genofile, "sample.id"))
print(head(sample_ids))

# ============================================================================
# STEP 3: DEFINE POPULATION GROUPS
# ============================================================================

# Define your sample groups based on heat tolerance
# Adjust these according to your actual sample naming/ordering
heat_tolerant_samples <- sample_ids[1:12]    # First 12 samples
less_tolerant_samples <- sample_ids[13:35]   # Next 23 samples

# Create population vector
population <- rep(NA, length(sample_ids))
population[sample_ids %in% heat_tolerant_samples] <- "Heat_Tolerant"
population[sample_ids %in% less_tolerant_samples] <- "Less_Tolerant"

# Create a sample information dataframe
sample_info <- data.frame(
  Sample_ID = sample_ids,
  Population = population,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Check population assignment
table(sample_info$Population, useNA = "ifany")

# ============================================================================
# STEP 4: QUALITY CONTROL AND SNP FILTERING
# ============================================================================

# Optional: Filter SNPs based on missing rate and MAF
# Remove SNPs with >10% missing data and MAF < 0.05
snp_filtered <- snpgdsSelectSNP(genofile, 
                               missing.rate = 0.1,     # Max 10% missing
                               maf = 0.05,              # Min 5% minor allele frequency
                               autosome.only = FALSE)   # Include all chromosomes

cat("SNPs after filtering:", length(snp_filtered), "\n")

# ============================================================================
# STEP 5: PERFORM PCA ANALYSIS
# ============================================================================

# Run PCA analysis
pca_result <- snpgdsPCA(genofile, 
                       snp.id = snp_filtered,        # Use filtered SNPs
                       num.thread = 2,               # Number of CPU cores
                       autosome.only = FALSE)        # Include all chromosomes

# Extract variance explained by each PC
var_explained <- pca_result$varprop * 100  # Convert to percentage
cat("Variance explained by first 5 PCs:\n")
for(i in 1:5) {
  cat("PC", i, ":", round(var_explained[i], 2), "%\n")
}

# ============================================================================
# STEP 6: PREPARE DATA FOR VISUALIZATION
# ============================================================================

# Create PCA dataframe for plotting
pca_data <- data.frame(
  Sample_ID = pca_result$sample.id,
  PC1 = pca_result$eigenvect[,1],
  PC2 = pca_result$eigenvect[,2],
  PC3 = pca_result$eigenvect[,3],
  PC4 = pca_result$eigenvect[,4],
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Merge with population information
pca_data <- merge(pca_data, sample_info, by = "Sample_ID")

# ============================================================================
# STEP 7: VISUALIZATION
# ============================================================================

# Define colors for populations
colors <- c("Heat_Tolerant" = "#E31A1C", "Less_Tolerant" = "#1F78B4")

# Plot PC1 vs PC2
p1 <- ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Population)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top",
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Principal Component Analysis (PC1 vs PC2)",
    subtitle = paste0("Heat-tolerant (n=12) vs Less-tolerant (n=23) genomes"),
    x = paste0("PC1 (", round(var_explained[1], 1), "% variance)"),
    y = paste0("PC2 (", round(var_explained[2], 1), "% variance)"),
    color = "Heat Tolerance"
  )

print(p1)

# Plot PC1 vs PC3
p2 <- ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = PC1, y = PC3, color = Population)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top",
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Principal Component Analysis (PC1 vs PC3)",
    x = paste0("PC1 (", round(var_explained[1], 1), "% variance)"),
    y = paste0("PC3 (", round(var_explained[3], 1), "% variance)"),
    color = "Heat Tolerance"
  )

print(p2)

# Scree plot - variance explained by each PC
scree_data <- data.frame(
  PC = 1:10,
  Variance = var_explained[1:10]
)

p3 <- ggplot(scree_data, aes(x = PC, y = Variance)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue", size = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "blue", size = 2) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Scree Plot - Variance Explained by Principal Components",
    x = "Principal Component",
    y = "Variance Explained (%)"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:10)

print(p3)

# ============================================================================
# STEP 8: CALCULATE GENETIC DISTANCES AND CLUSTERING
# ============================================================================

# Calculate pairwise genetic distances
dist_matrix <- snpgdsDiss(genofile, 
                         snp.id = snp_filtered,
                         autosome.only = FALSE)

# Perform hierarchical clustering
hclust_result <- hclust(as.dist(dist_matrix$diss), method = "ward.D2")

# Plot dendrogram
plot(hclust_result, 
     labels = sample_info$Population[match(hclust_result$labels, sample_info$Sample_ID)],
     main = "Hierarchical Clustering Based on Genetic Distance",
     xlab = "Samples", ylab = "Distance",
     cex = 0.8)




# 3. Introduction to renv.

RENV is a package management package. It makes a file relative to the R project. It saves it in a lockfile. Lockfile will help to load all the package and the current versions.

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
install.packages(“renv”)

#On the console. 
#accesses all the functions within a package.
renv::init()

Check activate.R within the renv file created.

.libPaths()
#tells you where the packages are installed.

renv::snapshot() #It's like the save button.

Renv::restore(). #install all the packages in the lockfile.
